WASHINGTON, July 3.-The pos
sible unconstitutionality of the arid
land reclamation act, as indicated in
a part of the decision by the United
States supreme court in the case of
the state of Kansas VS.. the state of
Colorado, will not cause any change
at this time in the plans of the
United States reclamation service.


1t is authoritatively announced
present exactly as in the past 4
years, even though a test case has
been started in Washington state.


The reclamation service has been
advised by the department of JUS
tice Brewer in that case are entirely
oicta. They were not necessary to
the decision of the case, and are
therefore not regarded as binding
All that the court decided In that
case, according to the lawyers, is
that no state has exclusive right to
control waters which enter another
state, and that the federal courts
viii administer an equitable divi-
sion of waters between states.


After deciding the point involved,
Justice Brewer went out of the way
to reply to an argument which had
been made by Solicitor General
Hoyt, who had asked leave to enter
the case to speak for the the inter-
ests of the federal reclamation
works. Gen. Hoyt had argued that
the federal government was superior
In all cases where the states had not
taken jurisdiction over the water. In
denying this doctrine, Justice Blew
err called attention to the fact that
the general government has only
such powers as are delegated to it
by the states. After raising the
question whether the power to re-
claim arid lands is one of the pow
ers delegated, he said:


1T is enough to say that no one
of them (the enumerated powers
granted the federal government (by
any implication refers to the recla-
nation of arid lands"


After discussing the grant of pow
ers by implication, he concluded on
that point;


1T is still true that no independ-
ent and unmentioned power passes
to the national government or can
rightfully be exercised by congress"
